The **San Diego Gulls** are a professional [ice hockey](/wiki/Ice_hockey "Ice hockey") team based in [San Diego](/wiki/San_Diego "San Diego"). They are the [American Hockey League](/wiki/American_Hockey_League "American Hockey League") (AHL) affiliate of the [National Hockey League](/wiki/National_Hockey_League "National Hockey League") (NHL)'s [Anaheim Ducks](/wiki/Anaheim_Ducks "Anaheim Ducks"). The team plays its home games at [Pechanga Arena](/wiki/Pechanga_Arena "Pechanga Arena").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Diego_Gulls&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

On January 29, 2015, the [Anaheim Ducks](/wiki/Anaheim_Ducks "Anaheim Ducks") announced that they would purchase the [Norfolk Admirals](/wiki/Norfolk_Admirals_(AHL) "Norfolk Admirals (AHL)") of the [American Hockey League](/wiki/American_Hockey_League "American Hockey League"), and move the team to San Diego as one of five charter members of the AHL's new Pacific Division. The team plays at [Pechanga Arena](/wiki/Pechanga_Arena "Pechanga Arena") in San Diego, the sixth professional hockey team to play there, following the [original San Diego Gulls](/wiki/San_Diego_Gulls_(1966%E2%80%9374) "San Diego Gulls (1966–74)") of the [WHL](/wiki/Western_Hockey_League_(minor_pro) "Western Hockey League (minor pro)") (1966–74), the [San Diego Mariners](/wiki/San_Diego_Mariners "San Diego Mariners") of the [WHA](/wiki/World_Hockey_Association "World Hockey Association") (1974–1977), the [San Diego Hawks](/wiki/San_Diego_Hawks "San Diego Hawks") of the [Pacific Hockey League](/wiki/Pacific_Hockey_League "Pacific Hockey League") (1977–1979), the [second San Diego Gulls](/wiki/San_Diego_Gulls_(1990%E2%80%931995) "San Diego Gulls (1990–1995)") of the [IHL](/wiki/International_Hockey_League_(1945%E2%80%932001) "International Hockey League (1945–2001)") (1990–1995), and the [third San Diego Gulls](/wiki/San_Diego_Gulls_(1995%E2%80%932006) "San Diego Gulls (1995–2006)") of the [West Coast Hockey League](/wiki/West_Coast_Hockey_League "West Coast Hockey League") (1995–2003) and [ECHL](/wiki/ECHL "ECHL") (2003–2006).[[1]](#cite_note-ahlsandiego-1) The current team is the fifth[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] hockey team in San Diego to use the "Gulls" name.

The Gulls' name, logo, and colors were revealed on February 22, 2015, at HockeyFest.[[2]](#cite_note-hockeyfest-2)[[3]](#cite_note-The_Gulls_Are_Back_In_Town-3) HockeyFest was deemed a success, drawing over 8,500 enthusiastic hockey fans.[[4]](#cite_note-Hockeyfest_a_Hit-4)

The San Diego Gulls played their first home game on October 10, 2015, against the [Grand Rapids Griffins](/wiki/Grand_Rapids_Griffins "Grand Rapids Griffins"). The team finished its inaugural season with an average attendance of 8,675, second in the league after the [Hershey Bears](/wiki/Hershey_Bears "Hershey Bears").[[5]](#cite_note-5)

After four seasons and three playoff appearances, the Anaheim Ducks promoted Gulls' head coach [Dallas Eakins](/wiki/Dallas_Eakins "Dallas Eakins") to the same position with the Ducks.[[6]](#cite_note-6) Former [Florida Panthers](/wiki/Florida_Panthers "Florida Panthers")' head coach [Kevin Dineen](/wiki/Kevin_Dineen "Kevin Dineen") was hired as the next head coach.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

Approaching the delayed [2020–21](/wiki/2020%E2%80%9321_AHL_season "2020–21 AHL season") season, due to [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_North_America "COVID-19 pandemic in North America") considerations, the Gulls announced they would temporarily relocate and play the season out of the Ducks' practice rink, [Great Park Ice & FivePoint Arena](/wiki/Great_Park_Ice_%26_FivePoint_Arena "Great Park Ice & FivePoint Arena"), in [Irvine, California](/wiki/Irvine,_California "Irvine, California").[[8]](#cite_note-8) The Gulls finished third in the Pacific Division and lost in the semifinals to the second place [Bakersfield Condors](/wiki/Bakersfield_Condors "Bakersfield Condors") in division postseason tournament. After two seasons, the Ducks did not extend head coach Dineen, instead hiring former [Laval Rocket](/wiki/Laval_Rocket "Laval Rocket") head coach [Joel Bouchard](/wiki/Jo%C3%ABl_Bouchard "Joël Bouchard").[[9]](#cite_note-9) After a lackluster season under Bouchard, the Gulls would hire long-time AHL head coach [Roy Sommer](/wiki/Roy_Sommer "Roy Sommer") to be their fourth head coach in team history. In Sommer's only season as head coach of the Gulls, the Gulls finished with the fewest number of points in the AHL. He retired at the end of the season.[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-carter-11)

Matt McIlvane was hired as the new coach for the [2023–24 season](/wiki/2023%E2%80%9324_AHL_season "2023–24 AHL season"), specifically due to his experience of working with younger players.[[11]](#cite_note-carter-11)

### Rivalries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Diego_Gulls&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Rivalries")]

#### Ontario Reign

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Diego_Gulls&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Ontario Reign")]

The Gulls consider the [Ontario Reign](/wiki/Ontario_Reign "Ontario Reign"), the [Los Angeles Kings](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Kings "Los Angeles Kings")' AHL affiliate, to be their chief rivals and advertise games as "Rivalry Night".

The rivalry has been fueled by each teams' respective [NHL](/wiki/NHL "NHL") affiliates, with Ontario being an affiliate of the [Los Angeles Kings](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Kings "Los Angeles Kings"), and San Diego being affiliated with the Anaheim Ducks. Both Anaheim and Los Angeles share an intense rivalry dubbed the [Freeway Faceoff](/wiki/Freeway_Faceoff "Freeway Faceoff").

Both fanbases of Ontario and San Diego are best known for traveling to away games at the other teams' respective arena, [Toyota Arena](/wiki/Toyota_Arena "Toyota Arena") in Ontario and [Pechanga Arena](/wiki/Pechanga_Arena "Pechanga Arena") in San Diego on numerous occasions throughout the respective season while firing off chants of "Go Reign Go" or "Let's Go Gulls" while inside the opposing venue.[[12]](#cite_note-Rivalry_Night_in_San_Diego-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13)

The teams faced each other in the [2016 division finals](/wiki/2016_Calder_Cup_playoffs#Division_finals "2016 Calder Cup playoffs"), where the Reign defeated the Gulls 4–1 in a best-of-seven series.[[14]](#cite_note-2016_Playoffs-14) San Diego then defeated the Reign in the [2017 Calder Cup playoffs](/wiki/2017_Calder_Cup_playoffs "2017 Calder Cup playoffs") 3-games-to-2 in the division semifinals. Ontario would one-up San Diego with another postseason series victory after sweeping San Diego 2 games to none during the 2021–22 playoffs.

#### Bakersfield Condors

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Diego_Gulls&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Bakersfield Condors")]

In what is more of a rivalry from teams of the past, the Gulls have shared a longstanding rivalry with the [Bakersfield Condors](/wiki/Bakersfield_Condors "Bakersfield Condors"), the affiliate of the [Edmonton Oilers](/wiki/Edmonton_Oilers "Edmonton Oilers").

Bakersfield, then known as the [Bakersfield Fog](/wiki/Bakersfield_Fog "Bakersfield Fog") from 1995 until 1998 when the franchise was rebranded as the Condors, and San Diego, were charter members of the [West Coast Hockey League](/wiki/West_Coast_Hockey_League "West Coast Hockey League") from its inception in 1995 until the league was absorbed by the [East Coast Hockey League](/wiki/East_Coast_Hockey_League "East Coast Hockey League") in 2003.

In the 2015–16 season, Bakersfield and San Diego had multiple fights and line brawls during a game played at [Mechanics Bank Arena](/wiki/Mechanics_Bank_Arena "Mechanics Bank Arena"). Each team has scored a postseason series victory over the other.

Notably, on May 3, 2019, in the Pacific Division Finals of the [2019 Calder Cup playoffs](/wiki/2019_Calder_Cup_playoffs "2019 Calder Cup playoffs"), the Gulls defeated the Condors 3–2 in quadruple overtime for the 1–0 series lead. [Maxime Comtois](/wiki/Maxime_Comtois "Maxime Comtois") scored the game winning goal 4:20 into the fourth overtime period, ending the game after five hours and ten minutes of play. San Diego would upset Bakersfield by winning the series 4 games to 2. Bakersfield played San Diego during the COVID-shortened 2020–21 Pacific Division playoffs, defeating San Diego 2 games to 1 in the best of 3 series.

#### Tucson Roadrunners

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=San_Diego_Gulls&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Tucson Roadrunners")]

The Gulls also share a rivalry with the [Tucson Roadrunners](/wiki/Tucson_Roadrunners "Tucson Roadrunners"), the AHL affiliate of the [Utah Mammoth](/wiki/Utah_Mammoth "Utah Mammoth") and formerly the [Arizona Coyotes](/wiki/Arizona_Coyotes "Arizona Coyotes")' affiliate. San Diego and Tucson's rivalry has been dubbed the "I-8 Border Cup" with the winner of each season's series between the two teams being presented with the "I-8 Border Cup Trophy", which has been in the possession of the Roadrunners since the 2018–19 season. As of the 2024–25, the two teams have faced each other 80 times during the regular season, with the Roadrunners holding a record of 43–34–2–1 against the Gulls. In the 2024–25 season, Tucson secured the series with a 5–3–0–0 record.
